title: How the body holds grief in the chest and what to do about it
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
That weight in your chest isn't just in your head 

 You know the feeling. That heavy, tight, sometimes crushing sensation right in the middle of your che...
htmlContent:
That weight in your chest isn't just in your head

You know the feeling. That heavy, tight, sometimes crushing sensation right in the middle of your chest. It shows up when you think about someone you lost. When you replay a conversation that went wrong. When you sit alone at night and the silence feels louder than anything.

I've felt it too. For years I thought something was wrong with my heart. I went to doctors. They ran tests. Everything came back normal. But the weight stayed. And here's the thing: it wasn't my heart that was broken. It was my grief. And my body was holding it right there in my chest, because it didn't know what else to do with it.

Grief isn't just an emotion that lives in your mind. It's a full-body experience. And the chest is where it tends to settle, like sediment at the bottom of a river. Stay with me here. I'm going to explain why that happens and what you can actually do about it. No toxic positivity. No "just think positive thoughts." Real stuff.

What happens to the chest when grief gets stored

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades studying how trauma and grief get trapped in the body. He talks about something called "incomplete defensive responses." When you experience a loss, your body wants to do something. Fight. Flee. Cry out. Protest. But most of the time, you can't. You have to sit in the funeral. You have to be polite. You have to keep it together.

So your body freezes that impulse. And where does it freeze? In the chest. Right around the heart, the lungs, the diaphragm. That's where your body holds the energy of the cry that never came, the scream that got swallowed, the collapse you couldn't let happen because someone needed you to be strong.

Think about it. When you're about to cry, what happens? Your throat tightens. Your chest constricts. Your breath gets shallow. That's your nervous system trying to hold back the flood. And if you do that enough times, your body learns to keep that tension as a baseline. It becomes your normal. You walk around with a chest that's half-closed, breathing in short little sips of air, wondering why you feel so heavy all the time.

Here's something wild. Your body can hold a trauma response for decades without you knowing it. You might think you've "dealt with" something because you can talk about it without crying. But talking about it uses the left hemisphere - language, logic, narrative. The trauma might still be living in your right hemisphere, in your body, in your implicit memory.

Pat Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy addresses exactly this gap. She noticed that clients could have years of talk therapy and still be triggered by the same things. Because the body hadn't been included in the conversation. The body was still bracing, still guarding, still waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Try this. Stand up for a moment if you can. Feel your feet on the ground. Now push gently against a wall with both hands. Feel the resistance. Feel your own strength pushing back. That's your body remembering that it can take action. That it's not helpless. That it has agency. Sometimes the body needs to complete a movement it was never allowed to make. That's not weird. That's healing.

Gabor Mate, who wrote extensively about the connection between stress and illness, describes this beautifully. He says that the body keeps score. Every suppressed emotion, every unexpressed grief, every moment you told yourself "I'm fine" when you weren't, it all lands somewhere. For many of us, it lands in the chest.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Mate walks through case after case showing how unprocessed emotion literally shapes our physiology. It's not woo-woo. It's science. And it's a hard pill to swallow if you've spent your life being the strong one.

Why the chest is ground zero for grief

Your chest houses your heart and your lungs. That's obvious. But what's less obvious is that your chest is also home to your vagus nerve, the main highway of your parasympathetic nervous system. Stephen Porges, who developed polyvagal theory, showed that the vagus nerve runs right through the chest and connects to your heart, your lungs, your throat, your face. It's the nerve of connection and safety.

When you're grieving, your vagus nerve is under massive strain. It's trying to regulate your heart rate, your breathing, your digestion, your ability to feel connected to others, all while you're carrying this enormous weight. No wonder your chest feels like it's in a vice.

I remember a period after my father died where I couldn't take a full breath. I mean, I could breathe, but it felt like there was a hand on my sternum pushing inward. I went to a yoga class and the teacher said "take a deep breath into your belly." I tried. I couldn't. My diaphragm wouldn't drop. It was like my chest had its own lock and I didn't have the key.

That's the thing about grief in the chest. It's not just emotional. It's physical. Your intercostal muscles between your ribs get tight. Your diaphragm gets stuck in a partial exhale. Your shoulders round forward. Your upper back hunches. Your whole thoracic cage becomes a cage in the literal sense, trapping the grief inside.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. It gives you concrete ways to work with your nervous system rather than against it. I wish I'd had it back then. It would have saved me years of just trying to breathe through the pain without understanding what was actually happening.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get enough attention. The way chronic stress changes your relationship with pain. When your nervous system has been in overdrive for years, your pain threshold shifts. Things that shouldn't hurt, hurt. Things that should hurt, don't register at all. Your body's volume knob is broken.

This is what the research on central sensitization shows. The spinal cord and brain start amplifying pain signals. Fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, irritable bowel - these aren't imaginary. They're the body's honest response to a nervous system that never got to complete its stress cycle. The energy that was meant for fight or flight got trapped. And trapped energy has to go somewhere.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've been told your pain isn't real, if you've been dismissed by doctors, if you've started to doubt your own body - I want you to know something. Your body isn't lying. It's speaking the only language it has. The question isn't whether the pain is real. The question is what your body is trying to tell you through it.

What to actually do about it

Here's the part where most articles would give you a list of platitudes. "Practice self-care." "Journal about your feelings." "Give yourself grace." I'm not going to do that. Because if you're like me, you've tried all that and it didn't touch the physical weight in your chest. What works is body-level work. Direct, physical, nervous system-level intervention.

First, you need to help your chest remember how to move. Your ribs aren't supposed to be rigid. They're designed to expand in three dimensions: forward and back, side to side, and up and down. Grief locks that expansion. So you need to gently, patiently, coax your ribs back into motion.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Lying on it lengthwise, with the roller under your spine, allows your ribs to open. Your shoulders drop back. Your chest naturally expands. It's not a magic cure, but it's a start. Five minutes a day. That's all. Just lying there, letting the roller support your back, breathing into the places that feel tight.

Second, you need to work with your exhale. Most of us who hold grief in our chest are stuck in a partial inhale. We're braced for the next blow. The nervous system stays in a low-grade fight-or-flight because it's waiting for the other shoe to drop. The exhale is what signals safety. When you lengthen your exhale, you tell your vagus nerve: "It's okay. You can rest now."

Try this. Right now. Breathe in for four counts. Breathe out for six. Do it five times. Notice what happens in your chest. For me, it often brings a wave of sadness. That's the grief moving. Don't push it away. Let it come. Your chest is finally releasing something it's been holding for years. Sit with that.

Third, you need to give your grief a voice. Not through words necessarily. Through sound. Your throat and chest are connected. When you suppress your voice, you tighten your chest. When you allow sound to come out, your chest opens. You don't have to scream. You don't have to wail. But you might need to make some noise. A long sigh. A hum. A groan. Whatever comes. Your body knows what it needs.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports muscle relaxation and nervous system regulation. When your chest muscles are chronically tight from holding grief, magnesium can help them let go. It's not a replacement for doing the emotional work. But it can make the physical piece a little easier. Wild, right? A mineral can help something grief has been holding.

The grief will keep moving if you let it

Here's the hard truth I've learned. The weight in your chest isn't going to disappear overnight. It took years to build up that armor. It's going to take time to take it down. And sometimes, when you start to release it, the grief gets more intense before it gets better. That's normal. That's the layers peeling back.

Janina Fisher, who works with trauma and the body, talks about how the body has its own timeline. You can't rush it. You can't logic your way out of a somatic pattern. You have to meet your body where it is, with patience and curiosity, and let it tell you what it needs. Your chest might need to shake. It might need to cry. It might need to be held. It might need to be still for a very long time.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your chest tighten? Did it soften? Did you feel a wave of something? That's your body talking. Listen to it.

I still carry grief in my chest. Some days it's lighter. Some days it's heavy again. But I've stopped trying to get rid of it. Instead, I've learned to make space for it. To breathe around it. To let it move when it's ready. To trust that my body knows how to process this, even when my mind doesn't understand.

Your chest isn't broken. It's holding something that needs to be felt. And when you're ready, it will start to let go. Not all at once. Not on a timeline. But in its own way. In its own time. Right?
